visits.text: Drive from Luxor to Aswan (220 km) with two major stops. First stop: the temple of Edfu, dedicated to the falcon god Horus, is the best-preserved Ptolemaic temple in Egypt—and the second largest after Karnak. Built between 237 and 57 BC, it took 180 years to complete. Its entrance pylon, 36 meters high, bears reliefs showing Ptolemy XII smiting his enemies before Horus. Inside, the hypostyle hall with 18 columns is intact, with its ceiling still in place—which is extremely rare. Plan 1 to 1.5 hours for the visit.

Second stop: the temple of Kom Ombo, spectacularly perched on a promontory overlooking the Nile. Its unique feature: it's a double temple, dedicated simultaneously to two deities—Sobek (the crocodile god) on the south side and Haroeris (Horus the Elder) on the north. Everything is symmetrical: two entrances, two sanctuaries, two series of reliefs. On the outer wall, don't miss the panel showing surgical instruments—one of the rare representations of ancient medicine. The small crocodile museum attached displays mummies of Nile crocodiles found in the area. Count on 45 minutes to an hour.

The temple of Philae, dedicated to the goddess Isis, is one of Egypt's most beautiful Ptolemaic temples—and its setting is unique. Built between 380 BC and the 3rd century AD, it stands on an island accessible only by water taxi (10-15 minutes from the Shellal dock). The temple's recent history is as extraordinary as the ancient: threatened with submersion by rising waters after construction of the Aswan High Dam, it was entirely dismantled stone by stone then reassembled on neighboring Agilkia Island between 1972 and 1980 as part of a UNESCO international campaign. Trajan's Kiosk, with its 14 columns all with different capitals, is Aswan's most photographed structure. Plan 2 to 2.5 hours including the boat crossing.

The Unfinished Obelisk, in the pink granite quarries of Aswan south of the city, is a giant obelisk abandoned during carving—probably commissioned by Hatshepsut around 1450 BC. Had it been completed, it would have measured 42 meters and weighed 1,200 tons, making it Egypt's largest obelisk. A crack discovered in the rock forced the carvers to abandon it. The site is fascinating for understanding ancient Egyptian extraction techniques: you'll see tool marks, trenches carved with chisels, and traces of wooden wedges used to split the stone. Quick visit—30 to 45 minutes suffice.

The Aswan High Dam (Sadd el-Aali), built between 1960 and 1970 with Soviet Union assistance, measures 3,600 meters long, 111 meters high, and 980 meters wide at the base. It created Lake Nasser, one of the world's largest artificial lakes (500 km long). The dam ended the millennial Nile floods that had regulated Egyptian agriculture since the pharaohs. The view from the top looks over Lake Nasser on one side and the Nile on the other. The stop is short—20 minutes—but the perspective is impressive.

Departure at 3am from Aswan for Abu Simbel—280 km of desert road (3 to 3.5 hours), in organized convoy. Arrival at sunrise at the temples, when the raking light sets fire to the four colossi of Ramesses II. The Great Temple, carved directly into the cliff around 1265 BC, strikes with its dimensions: the facade measures 33 meters high and 38 wide. The four seated statues of Ramesses II reach 20 meters each. Inside, a hypostyle hall of eight Osirid pillars leads to the innermost sanctuary, 63 meters into the rock. Twice a year, at the equinoxes (February 22 and October 22), the sun's rays penetrate to the back of the sanctuary to illuminate three of the four seated statues—an astronomical alignment calculated 3,200 years ago.

Right next door, the Small Temple of Nefertari—one of the rare Egyptian temples dedicated to a queen—is dedicated to Nefertari and the goddess Hathor. Six 10-meter statues adorn the facade: four represent Ramesses II and two Nefertari. Inside, the wall paintings showing Nefertari crowned by the goddesses are among the best preserved in Egypt. The fact that Ramesses built a temple of this scale for his wife is unprecedented in Egyptian history.

The story of the temples' relocation is as extraordinary as the temples themselves. In 1964-1968, facing the rising waters of Lake Nasser, an unprecedented UNESCO operation cut the two temples into 1,042 blocks of 20 to 30 tons each, then reassembled them 65 meters higher and 210 meters back on an artificial hill. The total cost—financed by 50 countries—was considerable. Look carefully at the joints between blocks on the colossi: the cut lines are barely visible. Explanatory panels trace the operation behind the temples.

activities.text: Arrival in Aswan in late afternoon. The city has a slower rhythm and a more Nubian atmosphere than Luxor—the border with historical Nubia passes here. Stroll through Aswan's souk, Egypt's most colorful: spices in pyramids (cumin, saffron, henna), woven Nubian baskets, fabrics and perfumes. Vendors are less aggressive than in Cairo. The souk stretches for several hundred meters parallel to the Nile.

First felucca ride on the Nile at sunset—Aswan is where this experience is most beautiful. The river is dotted with granite islands, golden light falls on the sand dunes of the west bank, and white felucca sails glide in silence. Count on 1 to 1.5 hours. Negotiate the fare before boarding—feluccas depart from the south corniche.

Felucca crossing to Elephantine Island, in the middle of the Nile facing Aswan's corniche. The island hosts a Nubian village with brightly painted houses—blue, yellow, orange—and ruins of a temple of Khnum dating from the 30th Dynasty. The island's Nilometer, a graduated stairway plunging into the river, was used to measure the annual Nile flood and calculate taxes since pharaonic times. If you have time, push on to Kitchener Island (Aswan Botanical Garden), just next door—a tropical garden created by Lord Kitchener in the 19th century, pleasant for a shaded break.

Return to Aswan in the afternoon. Enjoy the rest of the day relaxing—after the 3am wake-up, you'll have earned it. A second felucca at sunset, iced Nubian tea (karkadé) on the corniche, or dinner at a Nubian restaurant on Elephantine Island are good options for this last evening in Aswan.

visits.text: Drive Cairo → Bahariya Oasis (370 km, roughly 4 hours on decent paved road). The landscape evolves gradually: Cairo's sprawl gives way to farmland, then pure desert—rocky plateau, sand stretching to infinity, no habitation for tens of kilometers. The descent into the Bahariya Depression (2,000 km²) is dramatic: the desert suddenly opens onto a green oasis of palm groves and springs.

Check into Bawiti, the oasis's main town (around 30,000 people). If you arrive by early afternoon, start with the Black Mountain (Gebel el-Ingleez, the 'English Mountain')—a WWI British observation post atop a basalt hill. The 360° view over the oasis, palm groves, and surrounding desert is Bahariya's best. Time your ascent for sunset.

The Golden Mummies Museum in Bawiti displays part of the 1996 discovery—one of the late 20th century's major archaeological finds. A Greco-Roman necropolis of over 250 mummies, some covered in remarkably preserved gilded cartonnage masks. The museum presents about a dozen in climate-controlled cases. You'll also see amulets, jewelry, and funerary pottery. The visit is brief (30-45 min) but fascinating—these mummies date from when Egypt was under Greek then Roman rule, and the mummification techniques blend pharaonic traditions with Hellenistic influences.

The nobles' tombs of Bahariya, in Bawiti village, are open to visitors—notably those of Zed-Amun-ef-Ankh and Bannentiu (26th Dynasty, around 600 BC). The well-preserved wall paintings show scenes from daily life and the journey to the afterlife. The Temple of Alexander the Great, built during his passage through the oasis in 332 BC en route to the oracle of Siwa, lies in ruins but Alexander's cartouche as pharaoh of Egypt is still visible—a rare document.

Make a stop at the salt lake and golden sand dunes on the oasis's northern edge. The lake, dry part of the year, forms a dazzling white crust that contrasts with the ochre sand and blue sky. The bordering dunes are perfect for sandboarding or simply walking barefoot in warm sand.

activities.text: Soak in one of the oasis's natural hot springs—Bir Sigam or Bir el-Ghaba ('well of the forest'), surrounded by palms. The water bubbles up at 38-45°C, rich in iron and minerals. It's total relaxation after hours on the road. The springs are open day and night—a midnight dip under the desert stars is an experience unto itself.

Lunch at a local restaurant in Bawiti—simple but authentic cooking: grilled chicken, rice, garden vegetables, wood-fired bread. In the afternoon, walk through the oasis's irrigated palm groves—an ancient network of canals distributes spring water to gardens growing dates, olives, apricots, and vegetables. The contrast between the intense green of the groves and the scorched desert surrounding them never ceases to amaze.

days_plan.itin_title: Egypt Road Trip: Oasis Route & White Desert
Egypt Road Trip: Oasis Route & White Desert

places.name: Cairo

visits.text: Arrival at Cairo International Airport (CAI). Transfer to your hotel in downtown Cairo or Giza—allow 45 minutes to 1.5 hours depending on traffic, which is legendarily chaotic in Cairo. Take time to settle in and adjust to the city's heat and noise.

If your flight arrives in early afternoon, head up the Cairo Tower on Gezira Island—187 meters tall, built in 1961, it offers the best 360° panorama of the city. On clear days, you'll spot the Giza pyramids on the horizon. The Zamalek neighborhood, on the same island, is Cairo's most pleasant district for a first stroll—colonial villas, art galleries, quiet cafés along the Nile. It's a good decompression chamber before diving into Cairo's chaos.

Start with Saladin's Citadel, perched on the Mokattam heights 75 meters above the city. Built from 1176 by Saladin to defend Cairo against the Crusaders, it served as Egypt's seat of power for nearly 700 years. Inside, step into the Mosque of Muhammad Ali—nicknamed 'the Alabaster Mosque' for its interior alabaster cladding rising 11 meters high. Its central dome soars to 52 meters and its Ottoman silhouette dominates Cairo's skyline. From the terrace, views sweep across Old Cairo's minarets and, on clear days, to the Giza pyramids on the horizon. Allow 1.5 hours.

Head down to the Sultan Hassan Mosque, below the Citadel—a masterpiece of Mamluk architecture. Built between 1356 and 1363 under Sultan An-Nasir Hassan, it measures 150 meters long with its southern minaret reaching 68 meters, medieval Cairo's tallest. The interior impresses with its proportions: the central courtyard opens onto four iwans (vaulted halls), each dedicated to one of the four schools of Sunni jurisprudence. Opposite, the Al-Rifai Mosque (1912) offers striking contrast—same Mamluk style but built 550 years later. It houses the tombs of Iran's last shah and several Egyptian royal family members.

Al-Azhar Mosque, founded in 970 by the Fatimids, is Cairo's oldest mosque and home to the world's second-oldest continuously operating university. Its 12,000 square meters blend Fatimid, Mamluk, and Ottoman architectural layers—note the three minarets, built in 1340, 1469, and 1510 respectively, each in a different style. The white marble courtyard dates from the original construction. Visit late morning and you might see students studying Quranic interpretation under the arcades, as they have for over a thousand years.

If time permits, walk 15 minutes south of the Citadel to the Ibn Tulun Mosque—the world's third-largest mosque (26,000 m²), built between 876 and 879. It's Cairo's oldest mosque to preserve its original form. Its exterior minaret with spiral staircase, unique in Egypt, recalls Samarra's minaret in Iraq. Climb it for a plunging view of the inner courtyard and Cairo's rooftops. Right next door, the Gayer-Anderson Museum (two 17th-century Ottoman houses connected by a passage) deserves a 30-minute detour.

Coptic Cairo is a neighborhood most tours neglect—wrongly. The Fortress of Babylon, built by the Romans in the 1st century, shelters within its walls the Hanging Church (Al-Moallaqa)—one of Egypt's oldest churches, its nave literally suspended above a fortress passageway. Its carved wooden ceilings and Coptic icons date from the 13th century. The Church of Saints Sergius and Bacchus (Abu Serga), below, marks the traditional spot where the Holy Family took refuge during their flight into Egypt. Just next door, the Ben Ezra Synagogue (9th century) is Egypt's oldest synagogue—its genizah (sacred document repository) yielded thousands of priceless medieval manuscripts discovered in the 19th century.

The National Museum of Egyptian Civilization (NMEC), opened in 2021 in the Fustat district 10 minutes from Coptic Cairo, perfectly complements the GEM. Where the GEM focuses on Pharaonic Egypt, the NMEC covers the entire story—from early humans to the 20th century, including Coptic, Islamic, and modern periods. The Royal Mummies Hall, in the basement, presents 22 pharaonic mummies (including Ramses II, Seti I, Hatshepsut) under optimal conservation conditions—soft lighting, controlled temperature, an atmosphere of reverence. The experience feels more intimate than at the GEM. Allow 2 hours.

Early departure to Saqqara, 30 km south of Cairo—the necropolis of ancient capital Memphis. The Step Pyramid of Djoser, built around 2660 BC by vizier Imhotep, is Egypt's oldest pyramid and humanity's first monumental stone building. Six stacked mastabas of decreasing size form its 62-meter height. The entrance through the colonnade—40 pillars sculpted like papyrus—strikes with its modernity. Beneath the pyramid, a 5 km network of underground corridors descends 28 meters deep to the burial chamber. You can visit part of the underground passages for an extra fee. The site is far less crowded than Giza—take advantage.

Continue to Dahshur, 15 km south of Saqqara—an even quieter site. Here you'll see the evolution of construction techniques: the Bent Pyramid (105 meters, around 2600 BC), recognizable by its mid-height slope change—Sneferu's architects had to reduce the angle during construction to prevent collapse. Right beside it, the Red Pyramid (104 meters), history's first successful smooth-sided pyramid. It served as the direct model for Khufu's Great Pyramid. You can enter the Red Pyramid via a 63-meter corridor—the ammonia smell is strong, but the three interior chambers in red stone are worth the effort.

Detour to the Imhotep Museum at Saqqara before leaving—small but remarkable. The blue faience tiles recovered from inside Djoser's pyramid, the mummy of Merenre I, and Imhotep's wooden coffin are displayed here. It's a good complement to understanding the necropolis.

Morning flight Aswan → Cairo (1h15). Final free day in Cairo for shopping, revisiting a favorite site, or simply wandering. Zamalek is pleasant for a last stroll—art galleries, cafés, boutiques. Khan el-Khalili souk is also a good spot for final gifts if you missed it on Day 2.

activities.text: First Egyptian dinner in Zamalek or along the Nile Corniche. Egyptian street food is worth experiencing from day one: koshari (lentils, rice, pasta, spicy tomato sauce—the national dish), ful medames (stewed fava beans), ta'ameya (falafel made from fava beans). Corniche restaurants offer views of the illuminated Nile—a perfect introduction to Egypt.

Plunge into Khan el-Khalili souk, founded in 1382 by Emir Djaharks al-Khalili on the former burial ground of Fatimid caliphs. Over 900 shops crowd into a labyrinth of covered alleys—goldsmiths, spice vendors, hammered copper, perfumes. Walk up Al-Muizz Street, one of Cairo's oldest (10th century), lined with 29 UNESCO-listed Islamic monuments. Don't miss mint tea at El-Fishawy café, open continuously since 1773. The souk hits its stride late afternoon when the heat breaks and the alleys come alive.

Dinner in Old Cairo—Egyptian street food delivers: koshari (lentils, rice, pasta with spicy tomato sauce), ful medames (stewed beans), ta'ameya (Egyptian falafel made from fava beans rather than chickpeas). The hole-in-the-wall joints around Khan el-Khalili serve generous portions. For a more relaxed experience, several restaurants with terraces overlook Al-Hussein Square.

Walk through the Fustat district, Cairo's oldest neighborhood (founded in 641 by Arab conquerors, even before the Fatimid founding of Cairo in 969). Al-Azhar Park, created in 2005 on a former 30-hectare garbage dump, offers panoramic views of the old city and its minarets—one of Cairo's rare green spaces. A good place for a breather in a packed day.

Stop at Memphis, the ancient capital of the Old Kingdom, 3 km north of Saqqara. The Mit Rahinah open-air museum displays the reclining colossus of Ramses II (10 meters long, in limestone) and an 80-ton alabaster sphinx from the New Kingdom. Memphis was founded around 3100 BC by Menes (Narmer), the pharaoh who unified Upper and Lower Egypt—it remained the capital for over a thousand years. Today, almost nothing remains of the city itself, swallowed by groundwater and modern construction. The museum takes 30 to 45 minutes to visit.
Transfer to Cairo International Airport (CAI) for your return flight. The airport is 30-45 minutes from downtown—allow extra time due to traffic.

places.name: Edfu

visits.text: Wake on the Nile—the boat navigated overnight from Luxor and passes through the Esna lock in early morning (sometimes with a 1-2 hour wait depending on river traffic). Arrival at Edfu mid-morning. The Temple of Edfu, dedicated to the falcon god Horus, is the best-preserved Ptolemaic temple in Egypt—and the second largest after Karnak. Built between 237 and 57 BC, it took 180 years to complete. Its entrance pylon rises 36 meters high with reliefs showing Ptolemy XII smiting his enemies before Horus. Inside, the hypostyle hall with 18 columns is intact, with its ceiling still in place—extremely rare in Egypt. The gray granite naos of the sanctuary, which housed Horus's statue, remains in place. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours for the visit.

activities.text: Afternoon navigation on the Nile between Edfu and Kom Ombo. The cruise passes through a landscape of palm groves, sugarcane fields and Nubian villages on both banks. This is the moment to enjoy the boat's upper deck—tea, reading, birdwatching (herons, egrets, kingfishers). The late afternoon light on the Nile is extraordinary for photography.

days_plan.itin_title: Egypt Journey: Nile Cruise and Red Sea Beaches
Grand Tour of Egypt: From Fayoum to the Nubian Temples

map_keywords: Navigation Nil croisière Edfou Kom Ombo

places.name: Farafra

visits.text: The trip's most spectacular day—by 4x4 with a Bedouin guide. Depart Bahariya heading south. First stop: the Black Desert (Sahara el-Suda), a lunar landscape of volcanic hills blanketed in basalt fragments—you could be on another planet. The black stones crunch underfoot and conical mounds march across the horizon. Your guide leads you up one of the hills for a panoramic view of this surreal terrain.

Continue to Crystal Mountain—a rocky outcrop encrusted with quartz and calcite crystals that sparkle in the sun. It's not really a mountain but a ridge a few meters high, sitting in the middle of nowhere. Further on, Agabat Valley offers white rock formations sculpted by wind amid golden dunes—one of the Western Desert's most photographed landscapes.

The White Desert (Sahara el-Beyda) is the trip's highlight. White limestone formations, sculpted by millennia of wind erosion, rise like giant mushrooms, columns, animal heads—an open-air museum of natural sculptures. The white rock, which was once the bed of the Tethys Sea 50 million years ago, contrasts violently with golden sand and deep blue sky. At sunset, the formations shift from white to pink to orange—the spectacle is hypnotic.

Wake at sunrise in the White Desert—the low morning light gives the limestone formations long shadows and golden hues. It's the best time for photography. Bedouin breakfast at camp (bread, cheese, jam, tea), then a morning hike through the formations. Your guide will show you fossils of shells and coral embedded in the limestone—reminders that this area was a seabed millions of years ago.

Drive to Farafra Oasis (90 km south of the White Desert), the smallest and most isolated of the Western Desert's major oases—around 5,000 people. Farafra's old village, at the foot of a hill, preserves its traditional mud-brick architecture. The atmosphere is peaceful, almost timeless. Visit the small local museum and the palm groves irrigated by natural springs.

activities.text: Camp in the White Desert—your Bedouin guide sets up camp among the limestone formations, prepares a wood-fire dinner (grilled chicken, rice, vegetables, mint tea), and you sleep under the stars or in a tent. With zero light pollution, the desert's starry sky is more intense than you've probably ever seen—the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye from horizon to horizon. The silence is absolute. It's one of the trip's most memorable nights.

Swim at Bir Setta hot springs (spring #6), a few kilometers from Farafra—sulfurous water at 38°C, surrounded by palms. It's travelers' favorite relaxation spot after days of 4x4 in the desert. In the afternoon, enjoy the oasis's calm or explore the irrigated gardens growing dates, olives, and apricots.

days_plan.itin_title: Egypt Road Trip: Oasis Route & White Desert
Egypt Road Trip: Oasis Route & White Desert

map_keywords: White Desert Sahara el-Beyda camp Egypt

places.name: Fayoum

visits.text: Long drive from Farafra to Fayoum Oasis (around 310 km, 5 hours via the desert road and El Haiz). The landscape is monotonous but grand—desert plateau as far as the eye can see, then a gradual descent into the Fayoum Depression. Arrival in the oasis, with its lakes, palm groves, and cultivated fields, is a welcome relief after two days in the desert.

The village of Tunis, perched on a hill above Lake Qarun, has become a ceramic arts center. Several pottery workshops are open to visitors—you can watch artisans at work or try the wheel yourself. The terraces of village cafés and lodges offer panoramic views of Lake Qarun, Egypt's largest natural lake. In winter, flamingos and other migratory birds gather there.

Wadi al-Hitan—the Valley of the Whales, UNESCO-listed since 2005, 40 km southwest of Tunis village (1 hour by 4x4 on track). This open-air museum holds fossil skeletons of primitive whales 40 million years old. The largest skeleton discovered—a Basilosaurus—measures 21 meters long, with five-fingered front flippers and still-visible hind legs. These fossils are direct proof that whales descended from land mammals. A small modern museum traces the history of discoveries with an explanatory video. The site can only be toured on foot via marked trails—vehicles are banned in the fossil zone.

On the way back, stop at the Wadi el-Rayan waterfalls—Egypt's only waterfall, formed by flow between two artificial lakes. The flow is modest but the contrast between falling water and surrounding desert is striking. It's a good picnic stop.

activities.text: Sunset over Lake Qarun from Tunis village—the sky's colors reflect in the lake, with silhouettes of palms in the foreground. It's a moment of absolute calm after days of desert adventure. Dinner at one of the village lodges—simple, fresh Egyptian cooking.

Last afternoon in Fayoum—stroll through the irrigated palm groves, visit a traditional water wheel (sakieh), birdwatch at Lake Qarun if the season's right. Fayoum is rural Egypt condensed—far from crowds, authentic, peaceful. It's the perfect counterpoint to the desert days.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Egypt: From Fayoum to the Nubian Temples
Egypt Road Trip: Oasis Route & White Desert
Egypt Road Trip: Oasis Route & White Desert

map_keywords: Wadi al-Hitan Valley of the Whales Fayoum UNESCO

visits.text: The Grand Egyptian Museum (GEM), inaugurated November 1, 2025, is the world's largest archaeological museum dedicated to a single civilization—500,000 square meters total area, 100,000 objects displayed across 12 permanent halls covering seven millennia of history. It sits 2 km from the pyramids on the Giza plateau. Right at the entrance, the colossal statue of Ramesses II (11 meters, 83 tons, 3,200 years old) welcomes you in the atrium. The Grand Staircase, spanning six levels and 6,000 square meters, is lined with some sixty monumental statues with direct views of the pyramids through the glass facade. The complete Tutankhamun collection—5,398 pieces, many displayed for the first time—occupies 7,500 square meters alone. Count on a minimum of 4 hours, ideally 6 for a complete visit. Tickets are no longer sold on-site—book online.

After the museum, head to the Giza plateau 2 km away—a pedestrian bridge now connects the two sites. The Pyramid of Khufu, sole survivor of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, has stood for more than 4,500 years. The numbers are dizzying: 230 meters per side, 146 meters tall originally (138 today), 2.3 million limestone blocks averaging 2.5 tons each, for a total weight of 5 million tons. It was the world's tallest building for 3,800 years. If you go inside (extra fee), expect a narrow, steep corridor leading to the King's Chamber—10.5 m by 5.2 m in solid granite. It's physical and claustrophobic, but the experience is worth it. Arrive at opening (8am) to avoid crowds and heat.

The Sphinx, carved directly from a rocky outcrop on the plateau, measures 73.5 meters long and 20 meters high—the largest carved monolith from antiquity. Its face, probably that of Pharaoh Khafre (4th Dynasty, around 2500 BC), looks due east toward the sunrise. Don't miss the Panoramic Point (Nine Pyramids viewpoint) on the road that curves around the southern plateau—this is where you'll get all three great pyramids and the three smaller queens' pyramids aligned in a single frame.

activities.text: Sunset over the pyramids from the Panoramic Point or from one of the terrace restaurants on Fayoum Road that runs along the plateau. The golden light on the pyramids in late afternoon is the best photographic moment—tour buses have left and the heat is bearable. Some organize camel or horse rides around the plateau in late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Egypt Journey: Pyramids, Nile Temples & Pharaohs' Treasures
Egypt Journey: Nile Cruise and Red Sea Beaches
Grand Tour of Egypt: From Fayoum to the Nubian Temples

visits.text: Flight Aswan → Hurghada (1 hour) or road transfer (4-5 hours via the Eastern Desert). Arrive at the Red Sea coast—the contrast with the Nile Valley is radical: here it's turquoise blue as far as the eye can see, coral reefs and desert plunging into the sea. Check into your hotel and take your first swim. Red Sea water stays 24-28°C year-round, with visibility often exceeding 30 meters.

Boat excursion to the Giftun Islands—a marine national park 45 minutes by boat from Hurghada. The two islands (Giftun Kebir and Giftun Soraya) are surrounded by some of the northern Red Sea's most beautiful coral reefs. The water is turquoise and crystal clear, the white sand beaches nearly deserted. Snorkeling here is exceptional: parrotfish, napoleons, butterflyfish, stingrays, and with luck green sea turtles. Multiple snorkeling stops are planned at different reefs throughout the day.

Last day in Hurghada—several options depending on your interests. For underwater enthusiasts: a discover scuba diving experience (no experience required, full instruction, 1.5 hours total) lets you descend to 6-10 meters on a reef—an incomparable experience to surface snorkeling. The reefs around Giftun and Abu Ramada are perfect for a first dive, with weak currents and exceptional visibility.

Last morning in Hurghada. If your flight is late in the day, enjoy a final swim or early morning snorkel on the hotel reef. Hurghada International Airport (HRG) is 10-15 minutes from downtown—far simpler than Cairo. Direct flights to Paris, Lyon, Brussels, Geneva and most European capitals, as well as connections via Cairo.

activities.text: Decompression day after 10 days of intensive sightseeing. Enjoy your hotel beach or head down to Hurghada marina for a walk—restaurants, cafés and shops line the port. If you still have energy, take a tour of the old El Dahar quarter to discover authentic Hurghada—local souk, mosques, neighborhood life far from the resorts.

First snorkeling from the beach—most Hurghada hotels have a reef accessible directly from shore or from a jetty. The Red Sea harbors over 800 fish species and 200 coral species. Even from the beach, you'll see butterflyfish, clownfish, surgeonfish and probably a moray eel or two.

Full day at sea with lunch on board. Excursions typically include 2 to 3 snorkeling stops at different reefs—gardens of hard and soft corals, drop-offs, shallow lagoons. The natural lagoon of Giftun Soraya, with waters 0.5-1.5 meters deep, is perfect for beginners and families. The reefs on the south face offer more spectacular drop-offs for experienced snorkelers. Return to Hurghada late afternoon.

Alternative: excursion to Dolphin Reef (Sha'ab el-Erg), about 1.5 hours by boat north of Hurghada. A pod of spinner dolphins permanently resides in the area—chances of sighting are high, especially in the morning. Swimming in open water near the dolphins (without touching or feeding them), then snorkeling on surrounding reefs.

Late afternoon relaxation at the beach or hotel pool. Last sunset over the Red Sea from the marina. Hurghada is also known for its Eastern Desert excursions (quad, 4x4 safari, Bedouin dinner)—an option if you want to mix things up for this last evening.

Transfer to Hurghada Airport (HRG) for your return flight. The airport is small and quick—1.5 hours before flight is enough for international flights. If you're returning via Cairo, allow a comfortable connection (3 hours minimum) to absorb any potential delays.

days_plan.itin_title: Egypt Journey: Nile Cruise and Red Sea Beaches
Egypt Journey: Nile Cruise and Red Sea Beaches
Egypt Journey: Nile Cruise and Red Sea Beaches
Egypt Journey: Nile Cruise and Red Sea Beaches

places.name: Luxor

visits.text: Early departure to Saqqara, 30 km south of Cairo—the necropolis of ancient capital Memphis. The Step Pyramid of Djoser, built around 2660 BC by vizier Imhotep, is Egypt's oldest pyramid and humanity's first monumental stone structure. Six stacked mastabas of decreasing size form its 62 meters of height. The entrance through the colonnade—40 carved papyrus-shaped pillars—is striking in its modernity. Beneath the pyramid, a 5 km network of underground corridors descends 28 meters deep to the burial chamber. It's possible to visit part of the underground network for an extra fee. The site is far less crowded than Giza—take advantage.

Continue to Dahshur, 15 km south of Saqqara—an even quieter site. Here you'll see the evolution of construction techniques: the Bent Pyramid (105 meters, circa 2600 BC), recognizable by its mid-height slope change—Sneferu's architects had to reduce the angle mid-construction to prevent collapse. Right next door, the Red Pyramid (104 meters), the first successful smooth-sided pyramid in history. It served as the direct model for Khufu's Great Pyramid. You can enter the Red Pyramid through a 63-meter corridor—the ammonia smell is strong, but the three interior chambers in red stone are worth the visit.

Stop by the Imhotep Museum at Saqqara before leaving—small but remarkable. The blue faience tiles found inside Djoser's pyramid, Merenre I's mummy, and Imhotep's wooden coffin are on display. It's a good complement to understanding the necropolis.

Karnak is the largest religious complex ever built—over 2 square kilometers of temples, pylons, obelisks and sanctuaries erected over 2,000 years by dozens of pharaohs, from Senusret I (circa 2000 BC) to the Ptolemies. Arrive at opening (6am) to have the Great Hypostyle Hall almost to yourself. This forest of 134 columns, built by Seti I and completed by Ramses II, measures 103 meters long by 52 meters wide—it could contain Notre-Dame de Paris. The 12 central columns rise to 23 meters with capitals 3.5 meters in diameter. In the raking morning light, the reliefs carved in stone take on striking depth. Allow at minimum 2.5 hours for Karnak, 3 hours if you want to explore the corners.

Don't leave Karnak without circling the sacred lake (120 × 77 meters), where priests purified themselves before ceremonies. The obelisk of Hatshepsut, the tallest still standing in Egypt (29 meters, carved from a single block of Aswan pink granite), rises between the 4th and 5th pylons. At the end of the main axis, the sanctuary of Amun's barque—the temple's spiritual heart—preserves remarkably well-preserved wall paintings. At the entrance, 40 ram-headed sphinxes (criosphinxes) line the processional avenue that once led to the Nile.

The Luxor Museum, on the Nile corniche between Karnak and Luxor temples, is a small, remarkably designed museum—far more pleasant than Cairo's old museum. Among the major pieces: two royal mummies (Ahmose I and Ramses I), a colossal granite head of Amenhotep III, and the talatat wall—283 painted sandstone blocks from Akhenaten's Temple of Aten at Karnak, demolished by his successors. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours.

Wake at 4:30am for a hot air balloon flight over the west bank—one of Egypt's most spectacular experiences. The balloon lifts off at sunrise and soars over the Valley of the Kings, Hatshepsut's temple and the sugarcane fields along the Nile for 45 minutes to 1 hour. Reservations are made the day before—flights are cancelled in high winds.

The Valley of the Kings holds 63 tombs carved into the rock between 1539 and 1075 BC—New Kingdom pharaohs had abandoned pyramids in favor of underground tombs to escape looters. Your ticket includes 3 tombs of your choice. The most impressive for their paintings: Ramses IV (KV2), Ramses IX (KV6), Merenptah (KV8). Seti I's tomb (KV17), considered the valley's most beautiful, requires a substantial supplement. Tutankhamun's tomb (KV62), discovered in 1922 by Howard Carter, is small and its paintings less spectacular than others—the 5,398 objects of the treasure are now at the GEM in Giza. Arrive before 7am—there's no shade in the valley and the heat quickly becomes unbearable.

The Colossi of Memnon, two seated statues of Amenhotep III standing 18 meters tall each, guard the entrance to what was Luxor's largest mortuary temple—now vanished. Carved from 720-ton quartzite blocks each, transported from the Gebel el-Ahmar quarries near Cairo 675 km away. In antiquity, the northern colossus emitted a sound at sunrise (probably due to expansion of stone cracked by an earthquake in 27 BC)—Greeks and Romans came from afar to hear it. The stop is quick—15 to 20 minutes suffice.

Hatshepsut's mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari is backed against a 300-meter cliff that serves as its natural backdrop—the architectural spectacle is unique in Egypt. Three monumental terraces connected by ramps rise against the rock face. The first measures 120 meters deep by 75 wide. The temple, built in 15 years by architect Senenmut, tells on its walls the trade expedition to the land of Punt (present-day Somalia/Eritrea)—you can see the frankincense trees transported by boat—and the divine birth of the queen. Hatshepsut, the only woman to have reigned as pharaoh for 22 years, saw most of her representations hammered out by her successor Thutmose III after her death in 1458 BC. Arrive early—the temple is in full sun from 9am.

Continue to Medinet Habu—Ramses III's mortuary temple, less crowded than Karnak but whose painted reliefs are the best preserved on the entire west bank. The battle scenes against the Sea Peoples (circa 1178 BC) still retain their original colors—blue, red, yellow. The ceiling of the second portico preserves traces of vivid pigments after 3,000 years. The outer enclosure, inspired by a Syrian fortress (the Migdol), is unique in Egyptian temple architecture. If you only see one 'off-the-beaten-path' temple in Luxor, this is the one.

The Ramesseum, Ramses II's mortuary temple, is worth a stop for its monumental remains—notably a broken colossus of Ramses II that weighed over 1,000 tons and measured about 18 meters standing. This is the colossus that inspired Shelley's poem Ozymandias in 1818. The hypostyle hall still retains part of its ceiling with astronomical paintings. The site is less restored than Karnak, giving it a romantic ruin charm.

activities.text: Cairo → Luxor flight in late afternoon (1-hour flight, several airlines offer daily departures). Upon arrival, settle on the east bank of the Nile. If your flight arrives before sunset, head straight to Luxor Temple—it's magnificent at night, illuminated. The pylon of Ramses II, his two seated colossi of 15 meters, and the remaining obelisk (the second has been in Paris's Place de la Concorde since 1836) form the entrance. Amenhotep III's processional colonnade, 20 meters high, gives chills under the spotlights. The avenue of human-headed sphinxes, restored and reopened in 2021 after decades of work, connects Luxor Temple to Karnak over 2.7 km—you can walk along it in the cool evening air.

Late afternoon stroll along the Nile corniche between Luxor Temple and downtown. Luxor's east bank comes alive at sunset—feluccas glide on the river, restaurant terraces light up. If you haven't yet seen Karnak by night, the sound and light show (nightly, multiple languages) takes you through the illuminated temple—it's kitschy but the atmosphere in the hypostyle hall under the spotlights is worth it.

Free afternoon to recover from the 4:30am wake-up. If you still have energy, detour to Deir el-Medina, the village of artisans who built and decorated the Valley of the Kings tombs for 500 years. The small workers' tombs—notably those of Sennedjem and Inherkhau—are paradoxically better painted than some royal tombs, with colors of astonishing freshness. The site is little visited and gives insight into the daily life of the royal tomb builders.

If your budget allows, visit the Valley of the Queens and Nefertari's tomb (QV66)—considered the most beautiful tomb in all Egypt for the vividness of its wall paintings. Every inch of wall is painted with exceptional finesse—Nefertari guided by the gods, scenes from the Book of the Dead, star-studded ceiling in deep blue. The supplement is steep and visitor numbers limited, but it's a moment apart. The Valley of the Queens holds about a hundred tombs, several open with the standard ticket.

Late afternoon boarding on the cruise boat moored at Luxor. Cabin installation and first dinner on board facing the sunset over the Nile. The Luxor-Aswan cruise lasts 2 nights and 3 days, with stops at Edfu and Kom Ombo. Navigation happens mainly at night—you fall asleep at one stop and wake at the next.
